entities.label: Horstmann's 1888 Douce 114 edition

reviewNotes: German belongs to Horstmann's introduction and notes, not to the medieval work's primary language profile.

The witness census follows Schultze 2013: six complete manuscripts, two near-complete manuscripts, six physical excerpt witnesses, and Caxton's incunable. Witness inclusion does not imply direct use by Horstmann.
Negative online-facsimile findings are date-bounded to 2026-07-12 and do not prove that no private, reading-room, microfilm, or unindexed reproduction exists.

A complete provider-published selection is not necessarily a complete manuscript facsimile: Cambrai MS 255's two public IIIF objects are cached in full, while their ten images cover only selected leaves of the 63-folio codex.

A complete provider selection with zero target overlap can still document the public surrogate boundary: all four published MS Douce 322 images are cached, but no work_portion is asserted because none images ff. 20rb-25vb.

Technical exposure through an Image API is not treated as download permission: Add MS 37790 remains linked-only because the British Library's current collection list expressly says the restored items are view-only and offer no download option.
Access and rights attach only to the named entity or component; the public-domain medieval text, provider images, modern scholarship, digital encoding, and repository artifact are separate layers.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item CME00059, a keyed structural encoding of Karl Horstmann's 1888 Anglia article. Horstmann transcribed the anonymous Middle English Seven Points directly from Bodleian MS Douce 114, ff. 89v-148r; the article is not a critical conflation of the other witnesses it mentions. The work itself is an abridged, rearranged, sense-for-sense compilation from Heinrich Seuse's Latin Horologium sapientiae. A complete 774-page scan of Anglia volume 10 and a complete 704-canvas provider bundle for the near-complete CCCC MS 268 witness are cached as whole objects, with the target portions mapped inside them. For Cambrai MS 255, both complete provider-published selections are cached (eight decoration canvases and two other canvases), but those ten non-contiguous images are only partial coverage of the 63-folio codex. Digital Bodleian's complete four-image published selection for MS Douce 322 is likewise cached, but it has partial manuscript coverage and zero overlap with the target at ff. 20rb-25vb. BL Add MS 37790 has complete 487-canvas viewer coverage from front cover through spine, with the target at canvases 274-276; its images are not cached because the British Library currently designates the restored collection view-only with no download option. No public whole-object facsimile of the direct Douce 114 source was found; the Bodleian catalog and current reproduction-order routes are recorded.

catalogingSubject.label: The Seven Points of True Love and Everlasting Wisdom
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English devotional compilation conventionally called The Seven Points of True Love and Everlasting Wisdom: an abridged, rearranged, sense-for-sense adaptation of material from Heinrich Seuse's Latin Horologium sapientiae. It is not the complete Latin Horologium, Karl Horstmann's German editorial introduction, any one manuscript witness, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML file.

contentParts.label: Table of contents
contentParts.summary: Lists the seven points and frames the treatise as material drawn from the Latin Orologium Sapientiae.

contentParts.label: Translator's Preface
notes: The CME label 'dedication' is an encoding label; scholarship treats this unit as the Translator's Preface.
contentParts.summary: Dedication to a spiritual daughter and explanation of the compiler's audience, selection, rearrangement, sense-for-sense translation, and seven-point plan.

contentParts.label: Point I: Divine Wisdom
contentParts.summary: The name, properties, and love of Everlasting Wisdom, experienced in bitterness and sweetness; the encoded section begins with the compiled proem.

contentParts.label: Point II: Christ's Passion
contentParts.summary: Love of Jesus in his bitter Passion and the disciple's conformity to that love.

contentParts.label: Point III: Tribulation
contentParts.summary: Glad endurance of tribulations and adversities through the examples of Christ and his chosen lovers.

contentParts.label: Point IV: True Spiritual Life
contentParts.summary: Keeping a true spiritual life grounded in love of Jesus and avoiding what opposes it.

contentParts.label: Point V: The Art of Dying
contentParts.summary: Learning how to die and cultivating the desire to die for the love of Jesus.

contentParts.label: Point VI: The Eucharist
contentParts.summary: Christ's love shown in the sacrament of his body and the worthy reception of the Eucharist.

contentParts.label: Point VII: Worship and Spiritual Marriage
notes: The Douce 114 copy is defective at the end; Horstmann marks the missing final words and supplies a parenthetical completion.
contentParts.summary: Loving, praising, and worshipping God as Everlasting Wisdom, becoming Wisdom's disciple and spouse, and concluding prayer and explicit.

summaries.value: An anonymous early-fifteenth-century Middle English devotional compilation adapted from Heinrich Seuse's Latin Horologium sapientiae. A clerical translator addresses a spiritual daughter, explains that he omits material unsuited to her and other devout readers, rearranges and renders the sense rather than the words of the Latin, and organizes the result as seven points corresponding to the seven gifts of the Holy Spirit. The dialogue of Wisdom and the Disciple treats divine love, Christ's Passion, adversity, spiritual life, dying, the Eucharist, worship, and spiritual marriage.

Modern scholarship records six complete manuscript copies, two near-complete copies, six physical manuscripts containing excerpts, and Caxton's 1491 print. Horstmann's 1888 edition and CME00059 derive directly from Bodleian MS Douce 114, not from a conflation of the complete witness tradition. The English work is distinct from the complete twenty-four-chapter Latin Horologium.

notes: This conventional modern title distinguishes the Middle English compilation from Seuse's complete Latin Horologium sapientiae.
